finding.observed_behavior: Extract shared constants to a common module so a rename triggers an import error instead of a silent mismatch

## What it does

osoji audits your codebase for dead code, stale documentation, misleading comments, and semantic contradictions. It produces structured, actionable findings — and ships with agent skill files that automate the entire triage-fix-feedback loop. Stop wasting time working around the garbage accumulating in your project.

## Quick start

| `osoji diff` | Show documentation impact of source changes |
| `osoji stats .` | Token statistics for source vs shadow docs |
| `osoji report .` | Re-render last audit in a different format |
| `osoji export .` | Export observatory bundle |
| `osoji push` | Push bundle to osoji-teams |

finding.triage_reasoning:
Reality — the evidence sweep for 'ast' returned hundreds of unrelated hits across 409 files because 'ast' is the name of Python's standard library Abstract Syntax Tree module and also appears in identifiers like 'ast_keys', 'last', 'fastest', etc. No specific evidence establishes a clear producer-consumer relationship between `src/osoji/shadow.py` producing the literal string 'ast' and `tests/test_plugin_python.py` checking it.

The surrounding code for test_plugin_python.py (L1-11) only shows an import of `PythonPlugin` — no string 'ast' check is visible in the evidence.

Ecosystem convention — 'ast' is the canonical Python stdlib module name, which means its meaning is defined outside the project. Any use of 'ast' as a plugin type/kind identifier would primarily be referencing the standard terminology.

Significance fails regardless — the evidence does not support that a rename of 'ast' in shadow.py would silently break test_plugin_python.py in a way that wouldn't be caught by other means. All three predicates require evidence; here the Reality predicate cannot be established.
